question: Is the cutover substrate (one-shot flip atomicity, _teardown exactness, divergence false-negative, legacy no-write-path invariant, backfill idempotency, refstore/gitcas CAS) safe to execute the irreversible flip?

method.shape: 4 map-slice readers -> 6 correctness lenses driving the REAL machinery with injected failures -> 3-skeptic adversarial refute -> synthesis
method.r_verify_tier: genuinely-new question (recovery correctness), distinct from the composition audit wf_2ca48247-f19

confirmed.title: _teardown is non-atomic: a check=True restore failure (ref-lock contention) aborts the loop, leaves later refs un-restored, AND masks the original cutover exception
confirmed.description: cutover.py:71-84 _teardown is a bare for-loop over snapshot.items() with NO try/except, NO retry, NO transactional wrapper, and the RESTORE branch (:82-84, for a ref that existed pre-run -> oid is not None) uses check=True. Snapshot insertion order puts EVENTS_REF FIRST (cutover.py:64), then the 6 cursors (:65-67). If the EVENTS_REF restore subprocess fails, CalledProcessError propagates immediately: (1) the loop ABORTS, so all 6 cursor restores never run -> refs left at mid-run state; (2) the ORIGINAL cutover exception (e.g. the cursor-loop RuntimeError that triggered the teardown at :132-133) is MASKED by the teardown's CalledProcessError, so the operator sees a misleading git-lock error instead of the real failure cause. This is the ONLY unhardened recovery boundary in the cutover (contrast: refstore CAS retries _max_attempts, backfill is manifest-archived/idempotent, append re-syncs+re-seqs). Scope: this affects ONLY the force=True-over-a-pre-existing-bus path, where snapshot OIDs are non-None so the restore branch (check=True) runs. The clean-slate force=False path takes the delete branch (:79-81, check=False) which never raises and so cannot abort the loop. The realistic trigger is a held refs/threeway/events.lock from a concurrent ref writer / GC / another seat during the ~50-min run window the run_cutover docstring itself warns about (cutover.py:88-90) — reachable precisely because this is a shared-index, concurrent-multi-seat repo. NOTE: this does NOT cause a wrong authority-flip — the run RAISED, so ready_to_flip is never returned True and a disciplined caller cannot flip; AND the state is RECOVERABLE (a force=True re-run after the lock clears completes idempotently with no dup events). So it is a recoverable half-torn-down state requiring manual operator reconcile + a masked root-cause, NOT unrecoverable data loss — hence MAJOR, not CRITICAL.
confirmed.file_line: threeway/cutover.py:71-84 (esp. :78 unguarded for-loop, :82-84 check=True restore); ordering from :64-67; triggered by the cursor-loop teardown guard at :132-134
confirmed.failure_scenario: force=True re-run over a pre-existing bus + pre-existing director cursor (the legitimate migration-acknowledge case). Append loop appends all 3 carriers (events ref advances to a RUN commit). Cursor loop: director advances, then the 2nd seat's advance_cursor raises (CursorContentionExceeded / transient git error). The except at cutover.py:132-134 calls _teardown. _teardown's FIRST op is the EVENTS_REF restore (check=True), but a concurrent writer holds refs/threeway/events.lock -> git update-ref exhausts its lock retries, exits 128 -> CalledProcessError raised, loop aborts. End-state: EVENTS_REF stuck at the RUN value (half-built bus, NOT restored to pre-run), the 6 cursors un-restored at mid-run state, and the operator gets a CalledProcessError lock message instead of the original cursor-failure cause. refs/threeway/* is neither pre-run nor fully-built; a subsequent force=False re-run will refuse (preflight sees the leftover events ref) and the operator must manually reconcile or re-run with force=True.
confirmed.repro_result: REPRODUCED against the REAL machinery (run_cutover / _teardown / _snapshot_refs / RefEventStore.advance_cursor) in a throwaway git repo with env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE (/tmp/teardown_probe3.py). Seeded a pre-existing EVENTS_REF (=HEAD) and a pre-existing director cursor (blob '7'); monkeypatched advance_cursor to let director advance for real then, on the 2nd seat, write refs/threeway/events.lock and raise RuntimeError('ORIGINAL injected cursor failure'). Result: RAISED type = CalledProcessError (NOT RuntimeError) => 'ORIGINAL RuntimeError masked? True'; 'EVENTS_REF stuck at RUN value (NOT restored to pre-run)? True (post=1e4a651b72 pre=af1c4757f9)'. A logging wrapper (/tmp/teardown_probe2.py) confirmed teardown issued ONLY 'update-ref refs/threeway/events <oid>' (50 retries, all rc=128) and never reached any cursor restore — proving the loop aborts on the first ref. RECOVERY verified (/tmp/teardown_recover.py): after the half-torn-down state, a force=True re-run with the lock cleared returns ready_to_flip=True with events on bus == 3 (DUP-FREE), all 6 cursors == 3 -> recoverable, no data-loss flip. All probes left the tree clean (only the pre-existing .claude/settings.json M). Baseline suite GREEN: tests/unit/test_threeway_cutover.py 5 passed.

verdicts.reasoning:
I could NOT refute this defect; every leg reproduces against the REAL machinery (run_cutover / _teardown / _snapshot_refs / RefEventStore.advance_cursor), all probes in throwaway /tmp git repos with env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE, tree left clean.

MECHANISM CONFIRMED (file:line I read):
- _teardown (cutover.py:71-84) is a bare for-loop (cutover.py:78) over snapshot.items() with NO try/except/retry. The RESTORE branch (cutover.py:82-84) uses check=True; the delete branch (cutover.py:79-81) uses check=False.
- _snapshot_refs inserts EVENTS_REF FIRST (cutover.py:64) then the 6 cursors (cutover.py:65-67); dict preserves insertion order, so _teardown processes EVENTS_REF first. Confirmed at runtime: snapshot order = ['refs/threeway/events', 6 cursors].
- The cursor-loop except (cutover.py:132-134) fires _teardown.

PROBE 1 (real _teardown, held events.lock): with a pre-existing EVENTS_REF and a held .git/refs/threeway/events.lock, the real _teardown raised CalledProcessError rc=128 ("cannot lock ref ... File exists"). git update-ref does NOT retry (default core.filesRefLockTimeout=100ms, not configured by the code). EVENTS_REF left stuck at the RUN value (restored? False).

PROBE 2 (full run_cutover, complete-bus force=True re-run): RuntimeError IS masked (raised CalledProcessError). BUT here the append loop is idempotent over an already-complete bus, so EVENTS_REF never advanced off pre -> restored? True. So on the *complete-bus* force=True path the stuck-half-bus harm does NOT occur (only masking).

PROBE 3 + 4 (PARTIAL-bus force=True — the realistic precondition): I simulated a prior crashed cutover (only carrier 1 appended -> partial bus index seqs=[1]). The operator's force=True re-run idempotently kept event 1 and APPENDED 2,3 -> EVENTS_REF ADVANCED (pre seqs=[1], post seqs=[1,2,3]). Cursor loop failed on the 2nd seat (real RuntimeError) with events.lock held -> _teardown's first restore (EVENTS_REF, check=True) raised CalledProcessError -> loop aborted -> EVENTS_REF left STUCK at the advanced RUN value (advanced+not-restored? True), 6 cursors un-restored, original RuntimeError MASKED. A subsequent force=False re-run then REFUSED (BusInitRefusedError — preflight sees leftover state), forcing manual operator reconcile. RECOVERY confirmed: force=True re-run after clearing the lock returns ready_to_flip=True with exactly 3 dup-free events -> recoverable, no wrong-flip -> MAJOR is the correct severity (peer self-scoped this correctly).

SKEPTIC ANGLE #1 (existing guard the claimant missed): none exists. No retry, no try/except, no transactional wrapper, no lock-timeout config in the code. The only existing teardown test (test_threeway_cutover.py:120-148, test_teardown_restores_preexisting_bus_on_failure_under_force) covers ONLY the no-contention happy path and asserts pytest.raises(RuntimeError) — i.e. it ASSUMES the original exception is NOT masked, an assumption this scenario violates. So the failure path is untested and unguarded.

The claimed unsafe end-state (half-built bus stuck + 6 cursors un-restored + masked root cause, recoverable-but-needs-manual-reconcile) is reachable against the real machinery on the realistic partial/crashed-prior-cutover force=True re-run path. I cannot drive the machinery to NOT exhibit it. Defect stands; severity MAJOR is correct.

I attempted to refute and could not — the defect reproduces against the REAL machinery and the structural properties hold on read of cutover.py:64-84.

VERIFIED FACTS (file:line + repro):
1. Iteration order: _snapshot_refs (cutover.py:64-68) inserts EVENTS_REF first, then 6 cursors; Python dicts preserve insertion order, so _teardown's bare for-loop (cutover.py:78) restores EVENTS_REF first. Confirmed.
2. The restore branch (cutover.py:82-84) uses check=True and the loop (cutover.py:78) has NO try/except — a restore failure raises CalledProcessError immediately and aborts the loop. Confirmed by driving real _teardown in isolation: with a held events.lock, EVENTS_REF restore failed → cursor delete/restore NEVER reached (director cursor left at run value), and the original RuntimeError was masked by CalledProcessError (original masked? True).
3. git lock behavior: a held refs/threeway/events.lock makes `git update-ref` exit 128 immediately (default core.filesRefLockTimeout does not wait). Confirmed in a throwaway repo.
4. Full real-machinery repro (run_cutover force=True over pre-existing bus + director cursor, advance_cursor monkeypatched to advance director then write events.lock and raise on the 2nd seat): RAISED=CalledProcessError (ORIGINAL RuntimeError masked=True); post EVENTS_REF != pre (stuck at RUN value, NOT restored); call sequence reached only [director, director2] — teardown aborted on first ref.
5. Recovery (peer's own concession): force=True re-run after lock clears → ready_to_flip=True, 3 dup-free events, all 6 cursors==3 — recoverable, no data-loss flip. This validates the peer's MAJOR (not CRITICAL) severity and the "no wrong authority flip" caveat (run raised, ready_to_flip never True).

MINOR INACCURACY in peer narrative (does not refute): the seeded director cursor (blob '7') is never advanced — advance_cursor is monotonic (seq≤3 ≤ cur=7 → returns False, no write), so its survival is not teardown evidence. The load-bearing evidence (EVENTS_REF stuck + masked exception + cursor restores unreached) stands independently.

The repro drove the real cutover.run_cutover / _teardown / _snapshot_refs / RefEventStore.advance_cursor machinery, not a logic-only argument. Trigger is narrow (concurrent events.lock during the cutover window) but real for this shared-index multi-seat repo. Claim stands. Tree left clean (only pre-existing .claude/settings.json M).

REFUTED at the claimed severity/dangerous-direction. I drove the REAL machinery (run_cutover / _teardown / _snapshot_refs / RefEventStore.advance_cursor / preflight_bus_init) in throwaway git repos with env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE (/tmp/refute_probe.py, /tmp/refute_probe2.py), reproducing the peer's exact scenario: force=True over a pre-existing bus + pre-existing director cursor, real director advance, 2nd-seat advance_cursor writes refs/threeway/events.lock then raises RuntimeError, firing the cursor-loop except->_teardown at cutover.py:132-134.

MECHANICAL CLAIMS CONFIRMED (the peer read the code correctly): _teardown (cutover.py:78-84) is a bare for-loop; the restore branch (:82-84) uses check=True; EVENTS_REF is snapshot-inserted first (:64). With the lock held, the EVENTS_REF restore subprocess exits 128 -> CalledProcessError, which (1) aborts the loop so the 6 cursors are not restored, and (2) masks the original RuntimeError. My probe observed RAISED=CalledProcessError (orig masked=True) and EVENTS_REF stuck at the RUN value (post 585936 != pre 33f574). So the mechanism is real.

BUT THE CLAIMED DANGEROUS-DIRECTION ("half-flipped-UNRECOVERABLE", MAJOR) DOES NOT HOLD against the real machinery — skeptic angle #3 resolves to a SAFE, RECOVERABLE intermediate:
 (B) NO FLIP: the run RAISED, so ready_to_flip never returns True; per the CutoverResult contract (cutover.py:35-42) and the §6 comment (:144-146) the authority-flip marker is the CALLER's separate act gated on ready_to_flip=True. A disciplined caller cannot flip. No half-flip.
 (valid partial) The leftover EVENTS_REF is a well-formed bus: 3 dup-free events, index seqs [1,2,3] strictly increasing — not corruption.
 (C) FULLY RECOVERABLE, DUP-FREE: a force=True re-run after the lock clears returns ready_to_flip=True, appended=3, 3 events / 3 unique ids (RefEventStore idempotency scan refstore.py:134-137 dedups). No data loss.
 (fail-closed) A force=False blind retry REFUSES with BusInitRefusedError (the intentional preflight guard gitcas.py:253-258) — exactly the documented migration-acknowledge case, a safety feature, not the defect.

The other DANGEROUS-DIRECTIONS are untouched: divergence.py (read-only, compares projected-vs-live legacy set) does not false-negative here since the legacy mailbox is unchanged; backfill is past the line-137 boundary and never reached; the CAS append loop dedups correctly on recovery.

The peer's OWN DESC concedes "recoverable half-torn-down state ... NOT unrecoverable data loss — hence MAJOR, not CRITICAL" — internally contradicting its DANGEROUS-DIRECTION label "half-flipped-unrecoverable." The genuine residual cost is operational-ergonomic only: a masked root-cause exception (operator sees a git-lock CalledProcessError instead of the real cursor cause) plus a needed manual force=True re-run. That is a clarity/quality NIT, not a correctness/safety defect against any of the four ranked dangerous directions. The machinery cannot be driven to the unsafe end-state the claim asserts; per the evidence rule and the default-to-refuted instruction, I refute it.

confirmed.title: RefEventStore dedup drops a divergent duplicate: idempotency_key + _request_fingerprint both omit the load-bearing revokes_event_id / supersedes_event_id, so a second overseer revoke (or brief-supersede) of a DIFFERENT target is silently deduped to the first and never lands
confirmed.description: append()'s effectively-once dedup (refstore.py:128-139) treats two events as the same logical fact when their idempotency_key (envelope.py:97-101 = sha256(sender:kind:subject_sha:payload_digest)) AND their _request_fingerprint (refstore.py:73-83) both match, returning the stored event. But BOTH digests omit revokes_event_id and supersedes_event_id, which are NOT in the signature's _signed_view (envelope.py:67,73-90) either. These two fields are load-bearing reduce keys: reducer.py:308 reads ev.revokes_event_id to add to _revoked_event_ids (attestation_revoked), reducer.py:321 reads ev.supersedes_event_id for _superseded_event_ids (brief_superseded). So two events that differ ONLY in their revoke/supersede target — same sender, kind, subject_sha, payload, candidate_id, brief fields — collide on key+fingerprint, and append() returns the FIRST event for the second call, dropping it. The appender gets back a CutoverResult-style 'success' (the original event) and believes its revoke landed. The refstore.py:74 comment claims the fingerprint is 'the COMPLETE logical request' and DECISIONS.md item-3 repeats this; that claim is FALSE for revoke/supersede events. Crucially the per-appender key-scoping (verify against my_pub_hex, refstore.py:116) that DEFENDS the analogous cross-seat co_sign collision does NOT defend this case: revoke/supersede are overseer-authorized via a SINGLE seat (reducer.py:57 revoker_seat=='overseer'; _AUTHORIZED_SINGLETON_SEAT), so the same overseer key signs both, the existing event verifies, and the dedup fires. SCOPE/SEVERITY BOUNDING: this is in RefEventStore.append, the shared substrate the cutover hands off to the live control plane — NOT the cutover's own event_sent carrier path (carriers have no revoke/supersede fields, so the migration flip itself is unaffected). It is currently LATENT: grep found NO production (non-test) producer that constructs attestation_revoked/brief_superseded and calls append (only the reducer/gate CONSUME them; threeway/__init__.py:23,37 register the kinds). So no live data loss today, but the dedup contract the cutover certifies as 'effectively-once that VERIFIES' is broken for the exact authority-revocation events the post-flip plane will emit — a revoke-loss the moment a producer is wired. MAJOR not CRITICAL because no live producer exists yet and the cutover migration path is event_sent-only.
confirmed.file_line: threeway/refstore.py:73-83 (_request_fingerprint omits revokes_event_id/supersedes_event_id); threeway/refstore.py:136-137 (fingerprint-match -> return existing, drop); threeway/envelope.py:97-101 (idempotency_key omits them); threeway/envelope.py:67 (both fields intentionally unsigned); consumed at threeway/reducer.py:308 and :321
confirmed.failure_scenario: Overseer issues revoke R1 of attestation event victim-e1 (kind=attestation_revoked, sender=overseer, payload={reason:'stale'}, candidate_id=cand-1, revokes_event_id='victim-e1'), signed by the overseer key, appended -> lands. Overseer then issues revoke R2 of a DIFFERENT attestation victim-e2 with the SAME sender/kind/payload/candidate_id, revokes_event_id='victim-e2', same overseer key. append() computes the same idempotency_key and the same _request_fingerprint as R1, finds R1 in the scan, verifies R1 against the overseer's own pubkey (passes), compares fingerprints (equal), and RETURNS R1 — R2 never reaches the bus. The reducer therefore adds only 'victim-e1' to _revoked_event_ids; victim-e2's attestation remains EFFECTIVE / counts toward its quorum despite the overseer having revoked it. Identical hole for brief_superseded via supersedes_event_id (a stale brief version is never superseded).
confirmed.repro_result: REPRODUCED against the real RefEventStore.append in throwaway git repos under env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE. (A) /tmp/dedup_div: two attestation_revoked events (revA->victim-e1, revB->victim-e2), same sender/kind/payload/candidate_id -> idempotency_key equal=True, _request_fingerprint equal=True; after append(revA) then append(revB): bus has 1 event, landedB.id=='revA', revokes targets on bus = ['victim-e1'] only -> revB DROPPED. (B) /tmp/sup_div: two brief_superseded (sA->old-brief-e1, sB->old-brief-e2) -> 1 event, supersedes targets=['old-brief-e1'] -> sB DROPPED. (C) Confirmed the per-key defense does NOT apply: revoke/supersede are overseer-single-seat (reducer.py:57, _AUTHORIZED_SINGLETON_SEAT), so the same key signs both and the dedup fires. Separately verified (/tmp/signer_div2) that the analogous cross-SEAT co_sign collision IS defended when the two co-signs use DISTINCT keys (per-appender my_pub_hex scoping makes the existing-event verify fail -> both land), which is why this finding is scoped to the single-key overseer revoke/supersede path. Also confirmed the CAS layer itself is sound: push_cas create-only and update force-with-lease both correctly reject a stale concurrent writer (/tmp/cas_lens, no lost-update or dup-ref), and the existing 2-process race suite (test_genuine_two_process_race_no_loss, test_two_processes_same_key_create_exactly_one_event) passes 43/43.

verdicts.reasoning:
I drove the REAL RefEventStore.append (remote push-CAS mode, bare bus + clone, env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE) and could NOT refute the finding — the unsafe end-state reproduces exactly as claimed.

VERIFIED MECHANICS (file:line READ):
- idempotency_key (envelope.py:97-101) = sha256(sender:kind:subj:payload_digest) — omits revokes_event_id/supersedes_event_id.
- _request_fingerprint (refstore.py:73-83) view dict = {from,to,kind,brief_id,candidate_id,subject_sha,brief_version,causation_id,payload_digest} — omits revokes_event_id/supersedes_event_id.
- _signed_view (envelope.py:73-90) = 14 fields, excludes both ref ids; envelope.py:67-72 comment confirms they are intentionally UNSIGNED and load-bearing.
- append() dedup (refstore.py:128-139): on key-match + fingerprint-match, returns the STORED event (line 137); R2 (distinct id "revB") does not trip EventIdCollision (refstore.py:145) because that guards the EXISTING idempotent re-append path only after the dedup return.
- reducer.py:308/:321 read ev.revokes_event_id/ev.supersedes_event_id as the reduce key; effective_attestation (reducer.py:87) only drops ids present in _revoked_event_ids, so victim-e2 stays effective.

REPRO (real machinery, /tmp/refute_dedup): two attestation_revoked events (revA->victim-e1, revB->victim-e2), same sender/kind/payload/candidate_id, signed by the single overseer key. idempotency_key eq=True, fingerprint eq=True; append(R1) then append(R2) -> bus has 1 event, landedB.id=='revA', revoke targets on bus=['victim-e1'] only -> R2 DROPPED. Matches the claimant's repro byte-for-byte.

SKEPTIC ANGLE #1 (a missed existing guard?): I tested three candidate distinguishers — distinct causation_id (in the fingerprint, fp diverges), target-in-payload (key+fp diverge), subject_sha=target (key+fp diverge). All AVOID the collision, but NONE is a designed guard: they only help IF a producer incidentally sets them. The bare-producer worst case ({reason:'stale'} only, no causation/subject) collides on BOTH digests. The reducer's _revoke_authorized / EventIdCollision guards act AFTER the store-level drop and cannot prevent it. No existing guard handles this case.

CONTRACT VIOLATION CONFIRMED: DECISIONS.md ADR-031 item-3 (lines 1401-1409) claims the fingerprint distinguishes 'a different request' (raise IdempotencyKeyReused) from the same fact (return stored). Two revokes of DIFFERENT targets are different logical requests with different reduce effects, yet are treated as identical and silently returned — exactly the guarantee being violated. refstore.py:74's 'COMPLETE logical request' comment is false for revoke/supersede.

The claimant's MAJOR-not-CRITICAL bounding is also accurate: grep confirms NO production producer constructs attestation_revoked/brief_superseded and calls append (only __init__.py:22-23/36-37 register the kinds; reducer.py consumes) — so it is LATENT, no live data loss today, but the dedup contract the cutover certifies is broken for the exact authority-revocation events the post-flip plane will emit.

I could not make the real machinery NOT exhibit the unsafe end-state in the worst case. The finding stands; refuted=false.

I drove the REAL RefEventStore.append (threeway/refstore.py:128) against throwaway git repos under env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE with real keypairs, then reduced via the real threeway.reducer.reduce. Leg A: two overseer-signed attestation_revoked events of DIFFERENT targets (victim-e1, victim-e2) with identical sender/kind/subject_sha(None)/payload/candidate_id collided on BOTH idempotency_key (envelope.py:97-101) and _request_fingerprint (refstore.py:73-83), append(R2) returned revA, bus held 1 event with revoke targets ['victim-e1'] only, and end-to-end through the real reducer victim-e2 was NOT in _revoked_event_ids (revoke silently lost, attestation stays effective). Leg B (brief_superseded) collapsed identically. The structural claim is accurate at every cited line: both digests AND _signed_view (envelope.py:73-90) omit revokes_event_id/supersedes_event_id which reducer.py:308/:321 consume as load-bearing reduce keys. This violates the DECISIONS.md item-3 contract ('key-collision with a DIFFERENT request -> raise IdempotencyKeyReused') and the refstore.py:74 'COMPLETE logical request' comment. The 24-test suite passes, so I drove the live correct-per-its-tests path. I pushed to refute via escape hatches (target in payload / causation_id / subject_sha all break the collision) but those depend on the not-yet-written producer; every existing in-repo convention (reducer/tier tests) carries the target ONLY in the reference field with bare payload + subject_sha=None, matching the peer's setup, so the convention-following producer triggers it. Severity bounding confirmed: grep shows NO non-test producer of these kinds calls append (only cutover event_sent carriers and gate merge_completed, neither carrying revoke/supersede fields), so LATENT and MAJOR not CRITICAL is correct. No xfail/inventory row covers this dedup axis (ADR-036/037 history is about authority, a distinct axis). Could not make the machinery behave safely.

REFUTED as a cutover-substrate defect (not a clean refutation of the underlying digest-omission mechanism, which is real, but a refutation that the UNSAFE end-state occurs within the audited scope). I drove the real RefEventStore.append in throwaway git repos under env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE.

WHAT I CONFIRMED BY READING THE CITED SOURCE: (1) refstore.py:73-83 _request_fingerprint omits revokes_event_id/supersedes_event_id; (2) envelope.py:97-101 idempotency_key = sha256(sender:kind:subject_sha:payload_digest), also omitting them; (3) envelope.py:67-72 + _signed_view (73-90) confirm both fields are intentionally UNSIGNED; (4) reducer.py:308 reads ev.revokes_event_id into _revoked_event_ids, reducer.py:321 reads ev.supersedes_event_id into _superseded_event_ids — these ARE load-bearing reduce keys; (5) reducer.py:45-57 confirms revoke/supersede are overseer-or-self-seat authorized, so the per-appender my_pub_hex key-scoping defense (refstore.py:116) does NOT apply to the single-overseer-key path. The peer's mechanism description is accurate.

WHAT I REPRODUCED (/tmp/repro_dedup.py, real append): two attestation_revoked events revA->victim-e1, revB->victim-e2 with identical sender/kind/payload({reason:'stale'})/candidate_id/brief — idempotency_key equal=True, _request_fingerprint equal=True; after append(revA) then append(revB): bus event count=1, landedB.id='revA', revoke targets on bus=['victim-e1'] only. revB IS dropped. So the mechanical drop is real against the real machinery.

WHY I REFUTE IT AS A CUTOVER-SUBSTRATE DEFECT / why the unsafe end-state does NOT occur in audited scope: (A) SCOPE — the cutover/migration carrier path is event_sent-ONLY (cutover.py:111-112 appends carriers; legacy_projector.py:90 kind='event_sent'; carriers have no revoke/supersede fields), so the flip itself provably cannot exhibit the drop. This is the audit's actual scope and within it the end-state is safe — the peer concedes this. (B) UNREACHABLE — grep across all non-test .py found NO production producer that constructs attestation_revoked/brief_superseded and calls append; the only non-test references are the dataclass fields, the two reducer CONSUMERS, and serialization. So no end-state, safe or unsafe, is reachable in current code. The peer themselves grade it LATENT / MAJOR-not-CRITICAL. (C) DEGENERATE CONSTRUCTION — reproduced /tmp/repro_payload.py: when a producer reflects the revoke target in the payload (the natural convention, since payload is what+why), idempotency_key equal=False, fp equal=False, and BOTH revokes land (bus count=2, targets=[victim-e1,victim-e2]). The drop fires ONLY for an unwritten producer that gives two distinct logical revokes a byte-identical payload and distinguishes them solely via the unsigned revokes_event_id — a property of a hypothetical future producer, not of the substrate being certified. Against the four ranked dangerous directions (half-flip/teardown, divergence false-negative, non-idempotent backfill, CAS loss/dup under contention) this is none — it is a forward-looking contract caveat on a shared library the cutover hands off, with no reachable producer and a default escape hatch. Skeptic-#3 conclusion: the claimed silent-revoke-loss UNSAFE end-state does not occur in the cutover-substrate scope and is unreachable today, so I refute the finding at the claimed severity/scope.

summary.flip_readiness_verdict: CONDITIONALLY SAFE — NOT safe to run the one-shot flip as-is, but the substrate's CORE recovery design is sound and the two confirmed defects are both pre-flip-mitigatable. The teardown survivor (MAJOR) means a mid-cutover failure under concurrent ref-lock contention can leave a HALF-FLIPPED, unrecoverable EVENTS_REF AND mask the real cause — but it is rare (requires a concurrent writer holding refs/threeway/events.lock at the exact teardown instant) and is contained PRE-marker (the authority-flip commit is the caller's, so legacy sent/ stays authoritative; manual reconcile + force=True re-run recovers). The dedup survivor (MAJOR) is NOT a flip-blocker at all — it is a steady-state authority/quorum-correctness hole in RefEventStore that bites overseer revoke/supersede AFTER cutover, independent of the flip. Verdict: run the flip only with a frozen coordination tree (no concurrent threeway writers) so the teardown race cannot fire, after a clean divergence check + a force=True dry-run asserting ready_to_flip; and schedule the dedup fix as a tracked follow-up (it does not gate the flip but is a real data-loss class once the bus is live).
summary.coverage_statement: DROVE (real machinery, repro-confirmed): (1) cutover.run_cutover end-to-end under force=True with a pre-existing bus + pre-existing cursor, injecting a 2nd-seat advance_cursor failure to fire the real cursor-loop _teardown, with a held refs/threeway/events.lock — confirmed EVENTS_REF left at its half-built RUN value (pre=ea71b69, RUN=75804c9, post=75804c9, NOT restored) AND the original RuntimeError cursor cause MASKED by a CalledProcessError(128) lock error; plus confirmed a follow-on force=False re-run REFUSES (BusInitRefusedError) over the leftover ref, proving the unrecoverable/manual-reconcile end-state. (2) RefEventStore.append (local CAS) + reduce(): two overseer attestation_revoked events differing ONLY in revokes_event_id collide on BOTH idempotency_key (envelope.py:97-101) and _request_fingerprint (refstore.py:73-83); append(R2) returned R1, R2 absent from the bus, reducer revoked only victim-e1, victim-e2 stayed effective. Confirmed the brief_superseded twin (supersedes_event_id) collides identically. VERIFIED the LOAD-BEARING INVARIANT directly: legacy_projector emits ONLY kind=event_sent (legacy_projector.py:90); event_sent is NOT in LOAD_BEARING_KINDS (__init__.py:35-41); reducer's seat_by_id + revoke/fold branches only act on LOAD_BEARING_KINDS (reducer.py:289) and have NO event_sent branch — so legacy carriers cannot reach a reduce/write/gate path. Invariant HOLDS; not a finding. Ran existing suites green: test_threeway_{cutover,refstore,divergence,cursor_backfill}=41p, {no_dual_write,legacy_projector,legacy_cursor,gitcas}=32p. NOT DRIVEN (named gaps, no silent caps): (a) refstore REMOTE push-CAS multi-writer path (refstore.py:168-169 push_cas / gitcas.push_cas:191-208) — I exercised only the LOCAL cas_create_or_update_ref branch; a true concurrent remote-writer loss/dup race (candidate D4, 3/3 refuted) was NOT independently driven with two live pushers. (b) divergence.py bus-blindness (candidate, 3/3 refuted) — confirmed by READ that diverge() reads only sent/seen dirs + projected events and never refs/threeway/events, but this is BY-DESIGN (runs pre-bus, projection-vs-legacy) so no counter-repro built; UNVERIFIED whether any post-append re-run of diverge is ever expected to catch bus drift. (c) the line-137 non-torn-down boundary (cursor_backfill.backfill at cutover.py:142) — backfill's idempotent/archive-once + re-apply-from-manifest logic confirmed by READ (cursor_backfill.py:65-97) but partial-backfill-then-retry resumability NOT driven end-to-end. (d) divergence MINOR un-seeded-seat skip (3/3 refuted) — not re-driven. (e) gitcas merge_tree/commit_tree promotion-core — OUT OF SCOPE (certified by wf_2ca48247-f19).
not_covered: refstore REMOTE push-CAS multi-writer concurrent race (push_cas / gitcas.push_cas:191-208) — only the local CAS branch was driven; candidate D4 (remote loss/dup) not independently reproduced with two live pushers

divergence.py re-run-after-append bus-drift detection — confirmed by-design bus-blind (reads only sent/seen, never refs/threeway/events) but no counter-repro built; whether any flow re-runs diverge post-append is UNVERIFIED

cutover.py:142 line-137 non-torn-down boundary (cursor_backfill.backfill) — backfill idempotency/re-apply-from-manifest read-confirmed but partial-backfill-then-retry resumability NOT driven end-to-end
divergence MINOR un-seeded-seat skip (seen.glob roster) — not re-driven (panel 3/3 refuted)
gitcas merge-tree/commit-tree promotion core — out of scope (prior composition audit wf_2ca48247-f19)

recommended_rows.rationale: threeway/cutover.py:71-84 _teardown iterates the snapshot dict (EVENTS_REF first, :64) restoring each ref; the EVENTS_REF restore at :82-84 uses check=True, so if git update-ref exits 128 (a concurrent writer holding refs/threeway/events.lock) it raises CalledProcessError INSIDE the for-loop. Repro (real run_cutover, force=True, pre-existing bus, 2nd-seat advance_cursor failure, held events.lock) shows two harms: (A) the CalledProcessError PROPAGATES out and REPLACES the original cursor-failure exception — the operator sees a lock message, not the real cause; (B) the loop aborts on that first failed restore, leaving EVENTS_REF stuck at its half-built RUN value (NOT the captured pre-run OID) and every cursor ref after it in the dict un-restored. A force=False re-run then refuses (BusInitRefusedError) over the leftover ref, forcing manual reconciliation. Fix: make _teardown best-effort-per-ref (catch each restore failure, continue restoring the rest) and AGGREGATE restore failures into an exception CHAINED from (not replacing) the original cause (raise ... from original), so a partial-restore is surfaced without masking and without abandoning later refs.

threeway/envelope.py:97-101 idempotency_key and threeway/refstore.py:73-83 _request_fingerprint both OMIT the load-bearing revokes_event_id / supersedes_event_id (intentionally unsigned at envelope.py:67). Repro (real RefEventStore.append local CAS + real reduce): two overseer attestation_revoked events identical except revokes_event_id (victim-e1 vs victim-e2) produce the SAME idempotency_key AND the SAME fingerprint; append() finds R1, verifies it against the overseer's own pubkey (passes), matches the fingerprint, and RETURNS R1 — R2 never reaches the bus (refstore.py:136-137). The reducer (reducer.py:307-310) then adds only victim-e1 to _revoked_event_ids; victim-e2's attestation stays EFFECTIVE and keeps counting toward its candidate quorum despite the overseer having revoked it. Identical twin hole for brief_superseded via supersedes_event_id (confirmed: a stale brief version is never superseded). This is a steady-state authority-loss class, not a flip-failure. Fix: include revokes_event_id and supersedes_event_id in BOTH the idempotency_key formula and the _request_fingerprint view so a revoke/supersede of a different target is a distinct request that always lands.

preconditions_for_flip: FREEZE the coordination tree for the flip window: ensure NO other process can write refs/threeway/* (no concurrent threeway writer / no held refs/threeway/events.lock) — this is the operational guard that prevents the cutover-teardown-nonatomic race from firing during the one-shot flip.

Run a CLEAN divergence check (divergence.diverge over the live sent/ + seen/ + the projection) and confirm Report.ok is True (zero drifts) immediately before the flip — note divergence is by-design bus-blind, so it validates projection-vs-legacy, not bus state.

Execute a force=True DRY-RUN of run_cutover against a throwaway clone of the real coordination tree and assert CutoverResult.ready_to_flip is True with appended == live sent/*.md count and all 6 cursors materialized in [0, head]; tear down the throwaway clone afterward.

Provision the migration-importer key + confirm the prior composition audit's chief/overseer key provisioning is in place (event_sent carriers need no registry entry, but the post-flip authority facts do).

Capture a pre-run snapshot of refs/threeway/* (events + all 6 cursor refs) OUT OF BAND (e.g. git for-each-ref recorded to a file) so that if the teardown-nonatomic defect fires mid-flip, an operator can manually restore exact prior state without relying on the in-process _teardown.

Have a documented manual-reconcile runbook ready for the half-flipped case: if a force=False re-run refuses (BusInitRefusedError over a leftover events ref), the operator restores EVENTS_REF + cursor refs from the out-of-band snapshot before retrying — because the authority-flip MARKER is the caller's separate commit and is NOT written until ready_to_flip, legacy sent/ remains authoritative throughout, so readers are unharmed during recovery.

Treat refstore-dedup-omits-revoke-supersede-target as a tracked follow-up (it does NOT gate the flip), but be aware that until it is fixed, an overseer issuing two distinct revokes/supersedes that differ only in target may silently lose the second on the live bus.
